title: Regulating Act of 1773
bare_text: [Historical Act]
plain_english: The first step by the British Parliament to control and regulate the East India Company.
example: Established the Supreme Court at Calcutta.
extra_content: • Governor of Bengal became Governor-General of Bengal (Lord Warren Hastings).

title: Pitt's India Act of 1784
bare_text: [Historical Act]
plain_english: Distinguished between the commercial and political functions of the Company.
example: Created a new body called the Board of Control to manage political affairs, establishing a system of double government.
extra_content: • Company's territories in India were for the first time called the 'British possessions in India'.

title: Indian Councils Act of 1909 (Morley-Minto Reforms)
bare_text: [Historical Act]
plain_english: Introduced a system of communal representation for Muslims by accepting the concept of a 'separate electorate'.
example: Under this, Muslim members were to be elected only by Muslim voters. Lord Minto became known as the Father of Communal Electorate.
extra_content: • Satyendra Prasad Sinha became the first Indian to join the Viceroy's Executive Council.

title: Government of India Act of 1919 (Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms)
bare_text: [Historical Act]
plain_english: Introduced 'dyarchy' at the provincial level and bicameralism at the central level.
example: Provincial subjects were divided into 'transferred' (administered by governor with ministers) and 'reserved' (administered by governor without ministers).
extra_content: • First time direct elections were introduced in the country.

title: Government of India Act of 1935
bare_text: [Historical Act]
plain_english: A massive, detailed document that formed the blueprint for the Indian Constitution. It proposed an All-India Federation and divided powers into Federal, Provincial, and Concurrent lists.
example: Abolished dyarchy in provinces and introduced 'provincial autonomy'.
extra_content:
• Established the Reserve Bank of India.
  • Established the Federal Court (which later became the Supreme Court).

category: Part I: Union and its Territory
title: Article 1: Name and territory of the Union
bare_text: India, that is Bharat, shall be a Union of States.
plain_english: Declares the name of the country (India/Bharat) and describes it as a 'Union' rather than a 'Federation'.
example: Unlike the USA, Indian states do not have the right to secede from the Union.
extra_content: • The territory includes states, union territories, and any territories that may be acquired.

category: Part I: The Union and its territory
title: Article 2
bare_text: Parliament may by law admit into the Union, or establish, new States on such terms and conditions as it thinks fit.
plain_english: Allows the Indian Parliament to bring new, external territories into the country and make them States.
example: Sikkim was an independent kingdom but was admitted into the Indian Union under this article in 1975.
extra_content: • Article 2 deals with territories NOT already part of India (unlike Article 3, which deals with existing Indian states).

category: Part I: Union and its Territory
title: Article 3: Formation of new States
bare_text: Parliament may by law form a new State by separation of territory from any State or by uniting two or more States.
plain_english: The Parliament has the power to redraw the political map of India. It can create new states, change boundaries, or change the names of existing states.
example: The creation of Telangana from Andhra Pradesh in 2014 was done under this article.
extra_content:
• Requires prior recommendation of the President.
  • State legislature's views are sought but are not binding.
  • India is an 'indestructible union of destructible states'.

category: Part I: The Union and its territory
title: Article 4
bare_text: Laws made under articles 2 and 3 to provide for the amendment of the First and the Fourth Schedules and supplemental, incidental and consequential matters.
plain_english: Any law that creates a new state or changes borders under Articles 2 and 3 doesn't require a special Constitutional Amendment (under Art 368). It can be passed by a simple majority.
example: When Telangana was created, the Parliament passed the bill just like any ordinary law, without needing a 2/3rds special majority.
extra_content: • This shows the flexible nature of the Indian Constitution regarding state boundaries.

title: Article 5
bare_text: Citizenship at the commencement of the Constitution.
plain_english: Determined who automatically became an Indian citizen on January 26, 1950, based on domicile (residence) and birth.
example: If you were born in India and lived there in 1950, you were automatically granted citizenship.
extra_content: • Only applies to the situation at the commencement of the Constitution, not subsequent births.

title: Article 6
bare_text: Rights of citizenship of certain persons who have migrated to India from Pakistan.
plain_english: Gave citizenship to people who fled from Pakistan to India during the Partition.
example: A Hindu family that moved from Lahore to Delhi in 1947 was granted citizenship under this article.
extra_content: • Divided migrants into two categories: those who came before July 19, 1948, and those who came after.

title: Article 7
bare_text: Rights of citizenship of certain migrants to Pakistan.
plain_english: People who left India for Pakistan during partition lost their Indian citizenship, but if they later returned under a resettlement permit, they could regain it.
example: Someone who panicked and went to Pakistan in 1947, but realized their mistake and came back legally in 1948, could stay a citizen.

title: Article 8
bare_text: Rights of citizenship of certain persons of Indian origin residing outside India.
plain_english: Granted citizenship to people of Indian origin (PIOs) who were living abroad (in countries other than Pakistan) at the time of independence, provided they registered with the Indian consulate.
example: An Indian student studying in London in 1949 could register at the embassy to become a citizen.
extra_content: • Allowed overseas Indians to choose Indian citizenship.

title: Article 9
bare_text: Persons voluntarily acquiring citizenship of a foreign State not to be citizens.
plain_english: India does not allow dual citizenship. If you voluntarily become a citizen of another country, you immediately lose your Indian citizenship.
example: If an Indian actor acquires a Canadian passport, they automatically stop being an Indian citizen.
extra_content: • This principle continues today under the Citizenship Act, 1955.

title: Article 10
bare_text: Continuance of the rights of citizenship.
plain_english: Once you are a citizen, you remain a citizen, subject only to laws made by Parliament.
example: The government cannot arbitrarily strip you of citizenship just because they don't like you, unless Parliament passes a specific law allowing it.
extra_content: • Secures citizenship rights from arbitrary executive action.

title: Article 11: Parliament to regulate the right of citizenship by law
bare_text: Nothing in the foregoing provisions of this Part shall derogate from the power of Parliament to make any provision with respect to the acquisition and termination of citizenship.
plain_english: The Constitution only provided citizenship rules for people living at the time of commencement (1950). For all future citizenship rules, it gave absolute power to the Parliament.
example: The Citizenship Act of 1955 (and its recent CAA amendment) derives its authority directly from Article 11.
extra_content: • Citizenship is entirely a Union List subject.

title: Article 12: Definition of 'State'
bare_text: In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires, 'the State' includes the Government and Parliament of India and the Government and the Legislature of each of the States and all local or other authorities within the territory of India.
plain_english: Defines what the 'State' is, because Fundamental Rights are primarily claimed against the State, not private individuals.
example: If a private company fires you, you can't claim a Fundamental Right violation against them. But if ONGC or the Municipal Corporation does, you can, because they fall under the definition of 'State'.
extra_content:
• Includes statutory and non-statutory bodies like LIC, ONGC, SAIL.
  • Even a private body working as an instrument of the State falls under Article 12.

title: Article 13: Laws inconsistent with Fundamental Rights
bare_text: All laws in force in the territory of India immediately before the commencement of this Constitution, in so far as they are inconsistent with the provisions of this Part, shall, to the extent of such inconsistency, be void.
plain_english: Any law that violates a Fundamental Right is automatically null and void. This is the foundation of Judicial Review.
example: If Parliament passes a law tomorrow saying people of a certain religion cannot vote, the Supreme Court will strike it down using Article 13 because it violates equality.
extra_content:
• Expressly provides the doctrine of judicial review.
  • 'Law' includes ordinances, orders, bye-laws, rules, regulations, and even customs having the force of law.

title: Article 14: Equality before Law
bare_text: The State shall not deny to any person equality before the law or the equal protection of the laws within the territory of India.
plain_english: The government cannot treat people differently or unfairly. Everyone is subject to the same laws, and the law must protect everyone equally.
example: If a rich politician and a poor farmer commit the exact same crime, the court must judge them by the exact same rules without bias.
extra_content:
• Equality before Law: Negative concept (English Common Law).
  • Equal Protection of Laws: Positive concept (US Constitution).
  • Exceptions: President and Governors enjoy immunity under Article 361.

title: Article 15: Prohibition of discrimination
bare_text: The State shall not discriminate against any citizen on grounds only of religion, race, caste, sex, place of birth or any of them.
plain_english: The government cannot discriminate against you based on your background. This also applies to access to public places like shops, restaurants, and wells.
example: A government hospital cannot refuse to treat a patient because of their caste or religion.
extra_content:
• The word 'only' is crucial. Discrimination on other grounds (like educational qualification) is allowed.
  • Exceptions exist for women, children, socially/educationally backward classes, and EWS (Economically Weaker Sections).

title: Article 16: Equality of opportunity in public employment
bare_text: There shall be equality of opportunity for all citizens in matters relating to employment or appointment to any office under the State.
plain_english: Everyone gets an equal shot at government jobs without discrimination based on religion, race, caste, sex, descent, place of birth, or residence.
example: You cannot be disqualified from taking the UPSC exam just because you were born in a specific state.
extra_content: • Provides the constitutional basis for reservation in government jobs for backward classes (OBC/SC/ST) who are not adequately represented.

title: Article 17: Abolition of Untouchability
bare_text: 'Untouchability' is abolished and its practice in any form is forbidden.
plain_english: Completely bans the practice of untouchability. It is one of the few absolute rights with no exceptions.
example: Preventing someone from entering a temple or a public vehicle on grounds of untouchability is a punishable offense under the Protection of Civil Rights Act.
extra_content:
• The term 'untouchability' is not defined in the Constitution itself.
  • It is available against private individuals as well as the State.

title: Article 18
bare_text: Abolition of titles. No title, not being a military or academic distinction, shall be conferred by the State.
plain_english: The government cannot give people special royal titles (like Maharaja, Nawab, or Sir) to ensure everyone remains equal.
example: You can be called 'Doctor' (academic) or 'Major' (military), but the state won't make you a 'Duke' or 'Rai Bahadur'.
extra_content: • National awards like Bharat Ratna or Padma Shri are NOT considered titles under this article (Balaji Raghavan case).

title: Article 19: Protection of Six Freedoms
bare_text: All citizens shall have the right: (a) to freedom of speech and expression; (b) to assemble peaceably... (c) to form associations... (d) to move freely... (e) to reside... (g) to practice any profession...
plain_english: Gives Indian citizens six basic freedoms: speech, assembly, association, movement, residence, and profession. But they can be restricted for public order and security.
example: You have the right to peacefully protest, but you cannot incite a riot.
extra_content:
• Reasonable Restrictions: Found in Clauses (2) to (6).
  • Freedom of press is implicit in Article 19(1)(a).

title: Article 20
bare_text: Protection in respect of conviction for offences.
plain_english: Protects individuals from arbitrary punishment. You can't be punished for an act that wasn't a crime when you did it (ex post facto), you can't be punished twice for the same crime (double jeopardy), and you can't be forced to testify against yourself (self-incrimination).
example: If the government makes chewing gum illegal today, they cannot arrest you for chewing gum yesterday.
extra_content: • Cannot be suspended even during a National Emergency (Article 359).

title: Article 21: Protection of Life and Personal Liberty
bare_text: No person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law.
plain_english: The government cannot take away your life or your freedom unless there is a fair, just, and reasonable law that allows it.
example: The police cannot arrest you without following the strict legal procedures laid out in the CrPC.
extra_content:
• Maneka Gandhi Case (1978): Expanded to include 'due process of law'.
  • Right to Privacy: Declared a fundamental right here in the Puttaswamy Case (2017).

title: Article 21A: Right to Education
bare_text: The State shall provide free and compulsory education to all children of the age of six to fourteen years in such manner as the State may, by law, determine.
plain_english: Every child between 6 and 14 has a fundamental right to go to school for free.
example: The Right to Education (RTE) Act was passed to enforce this constitutional mandate.
extra_content: • Added by the 86th Constitutional Amendment Act of 2002.

title: Article 22: Protection against arrest and detention
bare_text: No person who is arrested shall be detained in custody without being informed, as soon as may be, of the grounds for such arrest...
plain_english: If you are arrested, you have the right to know why, the right to a lawyer, and the right to be presented before a magistrate within 24 hours.
example: The police cannot hold you in a cell secretly for days without telling a judge.
extra_content:
• Does not apply to enemy aliens or persons arrested under preventive detention laws.
  • Preventive detention cannot exceed 3 months unless an advisory board reports sufficient cause.

title: Article 23
bare_text: Prohibition of traffic in human beings and forced labour.
plain_english: Completely bans human trafficking, slavery, begar (forced labor without pay), and bonded labor.
example: A landlord cannot force a poor farmer to work on his land for free to pay off a generational debt.
extra_content:
• Available to both citizens and non-citizens.
  • Exception: The State can impose compulsory service for public purposes (like military conscription).

title: Article 24
bare_text: Prohibition of employment of children in factories, etc.
plain_english: Children under the age of 14 cannot be employed in any hazardous factories, mines, or construction work.
example: A firecracker factory is legally barred from hiring a 12-year-old child.
extra_content: • Reinforced by the Child Labour (Prohibition and Regulation) Act, 1986.

title: Article 25: Freedom of Religion
bare_text: Subject to public order, morality and health... all persons are equally entitled to freedom of conscience and the right freely to profess, practice and propagate religion.
plain_english: You can believe in any god (or no god), practice your rituals, and tell others about your religion, as long as it doesn't disturb public order, health, or morality.
example: Sikhs wearing and carrying kirpans is considered part of the profession of the Sikh religion under this article.
extra_content:
• 'Propagate' does not include the right to forcibly convert another person.
  • Available to both citizens and non-citizens.

title: Article 26
bare_text: Freedom to manage religious affairs.
plain_english: Religious denominations (groups) have the right to establish institutions for religious/charitable purposes, manage their own affairs, and own property.
example: The Ramakrishna Mission or a Waqf Board has the constitutional right to run their own religious schools and manage their donation funds.
extra_content:
• Article 25 protects individual rights, whereas Article 26 protects collective/group rights.
  • Subject to public order, morality, and health.

title: Article 27
bare_text: Freedom as to payment of taxes for promotion of any particular religion.
plain_english: The government cannot force you to pay taxes that will be specifically used to fund or promote any one particular religion.
example: The state cannot levy a 'temple tax' or a 'mosque tax' on the general public.
extra_content: • Prohibits the levying of a tax, but does NOT prohibit the levying of a 'fee' for secular administration of religious institutions.

title: Article 28
bare_text: Freedom as to attendance at religious instruction or religious worship in certain educational institutions.
plain_english: No religious instruction can be provided in schools that are wholly maintained by government funds. In other schools, students can't be forced to participate in religious prayers without consent.
example: A government-run Kendriya Vidyalaya cannot force students to read the Bible or the Quran during class hours.
extra_content: • Applies differently depending on who funds and administers the institution.

title: Article 29
bare_text: Protection of interests of minorities.
plain_english: Any section of citizens residing in India having a distinct language, script, or culture has the absolute right to conserve it.
example: People speaking a rare tribal language have the right to preserve their dialect without government interference.
extra_content:
• Available to ANY section of citizens, not just minorities (despite the title).
  • No one can be denied admission to state-funded educational institutions based purely on religion, race, caste, or language.

title: Article 30
bare_text: Right of minorities to establish and administer educational institutions.
plain_english: Religious and linguistic minorities have the right to set up and run their own schools and colleges.
example: A Christian minority community can establish a college (like St. Stephen's) and retain control over its administration and some admissions.
extra_content:
• The term 'minority' is not defined in the Constitution.
  • Only extends to religious and linguistic minorities, not cultural or social.

title: Article 31
bare_text: Compulsory acquisition of property (Repealed).
plain_english: This originally contained the Fundamental Right to Property. It was highly controversial and led to many court battles.
example: If the government took your land to build a dam, they had to pay you compensation based on this article.
extra_content:
• **Repealed** by the 44th Amendment Act, 1978.
  • Right to property is now a legal/constitutional right under Article 300A, but no longer a fundamental right.

title: Article 31A
bare_text: [Official Text for Article 31A will be synchronized with the Ministry of Law and Justice database.]
plain_english: The plain English explanation for this specific article is currently being drafted and verified by our legal experts.

title: Article 31B
bare_text: [Official Text for Article 31B will be synchronized with the Ministry of Law and Justice database.]
plain_english: The plain English explanation for this specific article is currently being drafted and verified by our legal experts.

title: Article 31C
bare_text: [Official Text for Article 31C will be synchronized with the Ministry of Law and Justice database.]
plain_english: The plain English explanation for this specific article is currently being drafted and verified by our legal experts.

title: Article 32: Remedies for Enforcement of Rights (Writs)
bare_text: The right to move the Supreme Court by appropriate proceedings for the enforcement of the rights conferred by this Part is guaranteed.
plain_english: If your Fundamental Rights are violated, you can go directly to the Supreme Court. The SC acts as the guarantor of your rights.
example: If someone is illegally detained, their family can file a Habeas Corpus writ directly in the Supreme Court.
extra_content:
• Dr. B.R. Ambedkar called it the 'heart and soul' of the Constitution.
  • SC can issue writs: Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Prohibition, Certiorari, Quo-Warranto.

title: Article 33
bare_text: Power of Parliament to modify the rights conferred by this Part in their application to Forces, etc.
plain_english: Parliament can restrict or completely remove the Fundamental Rights of soldiers, police officers, and intelligence personnel to ensure discipline.
example: An army soldier on the border cannot invoke Article 19 (freedom of speech) to publicly criticize their commanding officer or refuse an order.
extra_content:
• Only Parliament can make these restricting laws, not state legislatures.
  • E.g., Army Act, Navy Act, Police Forces (Restriction of Rights) Act.

title: Article 34
bare_text: Restriction on rights conferred by this Part while martial law is in force in any area.
plain_english: When Martial Law (military rule) is imposed in an area due to extreme breakdown of order, Fundamental Rights are heavily restricted, and government officials get immunity for their actions.
example: If an insurgency breaks out and martial law is declared, the military takes over civil administration and standard rights are suspended.
extra_content:
• 'Martial Law' is not defined in the Constitution.
  • Different from National Emergency (Article 352).

title: Article 35
bare_text: Legislation to give effect to the provisions of this Part.
plain_english: Only the Central Parliament (not state assemblies) can pass laws to enforce Fundamental Rights or prescribe punishments for violating them.
example: Only Parliament can pass a law prescribing punishment for untouchability (Article 17), ensuring uniformity across the country.
extra_content: • Ensures that fundamental rights are uniform throughout India.

title: Article 36
bare_text: In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires, 'the State' has the same meaning as in Part III.
plain_english: Defines the 'State' for the purpose of Directive Principles. It's exactly the same definition used for Fundamental Rights (Article 12).
example: If a government corporation (like LIC) makes a policy, it should ideally keep the Directive Principles in mind.
extra_content: • Links Part III and Part IV functionally.

title: Article 37
bare_text: Application of the principles contained in this Part.
plain_english: Directive Principles are not legally enforceable by any court. However, they are fundamental in the governance of the country.
example: You cannot sue the government in court if they fail to provide you with equal pay for equal work, because it's a DPSP, not a Fundamental Right.
extra_content: • They act as a moral compass for the government.

title: Article 38
bare_text: State to secure a social order for the promotion of welfare of the people.
plain_english: The overarching goal of DPSPs: The government must strive to promote the welfare of the people by securing social, economic, and political justice.
example: Creating policies that reduce the gap between the ultra-rich and the poor.

title: Article 39A: Equal justice and free legal aid
bare_text: The State shall secure that the operation of the legal system promotes justice... and shall, in particular, provide free legal aid...
plain_english: The government must provide a free lawyer to poor citizens so that justice isn't denied due to lack of money.
example: The Legal Services Authorities Act (NALSA) provides free legal services to the weaker sections.
extra_content:
• Added by the 42nd Amendment Act (1976).
  • It's a socialist principle.

title: Article 39
bare_text: Certain principles of policy to be followed by the State.
plain_english: Specific socialist directives, including adequate means of livelihood, equitable distribution of resources, prevention of wealth concentration, and equal pay for equal work.
example: The Equal Remuneration Act was passed specifically to enforce the 'equal pay for equal work for both men and women' clause of this article.
extra_content: • Article 39(b) and (c) are the only DPSPs that can override Fundamental Rights under Article 14 and 19 (as per Article 31C).

title: Article 40: Organization of village panchayats
bare_text: The State shall take steps to organize village panchayats and endow them with such powers and authority as may be necessary...
plain_english: The government should create local village councils (Panchayats) and give them the power to govern themselves.
example: This directive was finally realized via the 73rd Constitutional Amendment in 1992.
extra_content: • This is a Gandhian principle of democratic decentralization.

title: Article 41
bare_text: Right to work, to education and to public assistance in certain cases.
plain_english: The state should secure the right to work, education, and public assistance in cases of unemployment, old age, sickness, and disablement.
example: The MGNREGA scheme (right to work) and Old Age Pension schemes are direct implementations of this article.
extra_content: • Subject to the economic capacity of the state.

title: Article 42
bare_text: Provision for just and humane conditions of work and maternity relief.
plain_english: The government must ensure that workplaces are safe and humane, and must provide maternity leave for pregnant women.
example: The Maternity Benefit Act mandating 26 weeks of paid leave for women.

title: Article 43
bare_text: Living wage, etc., for workers.
plain_english: The state should ensure a 'living wage' (not just a minimum wage) and decent standard of life for all workers, and promote cottage industries.
example: Setting up the Khadi and Village Industries Commission to promote rural cottage industries.

title: Article 43A
bare_text: Participation of workers in management of industries.
plain_english: Workers should be given a say in how the factories or companies they work in are managed.
example: Appointing a labor union representative to the Board of Directors of a public sector company.

title: Article 43B
bare_text: Promotion of co-operative societies.
plain_english: The state should promote the voluntary formation, autonomous functioning, and democratic control of cooperative societies.
example: Government support for milk cooperatives like Amul.

title: Article 44: Uniform Civil Code (UCC)
bare_text: The State shall endeavor to secure for the citizens a uniform civil code throughout the territory of India.
plain_english: The government should try to create a single set of personal laws (marriage, divorce, inheritance) that applies to all citizens, regardless of religion.
example: Currently, different religions have their own personal laws (Hindu Marriage Act, Muslim Personal Law). A UCC would replace them with one secular law.
extra_content:
• Goa is currently the only state with a form of uniform civil code.
  • Highly debated topic in current affairs.

title: Article 45
bare_text: Provision for early childhood care and education to children below the age of six years.
plain_english: The state should try to provide early childhood care and education for all children until they turn six.
example: The Anganwadi system provides nutrition and pre-school education to toddlers.
extra_content: • Originally covered children up to 14. The 86th Amendment moved 6-14 years to Article 21A (Fundamental Right) and changed this article to 0-6 years.

title: Article 46
bare_text: Promotion of educational and economic interests of Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes and other weaker sections.
plain_english: The government must actively promote the educational and economic interests of SCs, STs, and weaker sections, and protect them from social injustice.
example: Setting up Eklavya Model Residential Schools for tribal students.
extra_content: • Forms the basis for various affirmative action policies outside of public employment.

title: Article 47
bare_text: Duty of the State to raise the level of nutrition and the standard of living and to improve public health.
plain_english: The state must improve public health and nutrition, and try to ban intoxicating drinks and harmful drugs.
example: States like Gujarat and Bihar banning the sale of alcohol (Prohibition) to fulfill this directive.

title: Article 48
bare_text: Organisation of agriculture and animal husbandry.
plain_english: The state should modernize agriculture and prohibit the slaughter of cows, calves, and other milch/draught cattle.
example: Various state laws banning cow slaughter draw their constitutional backing from this article.

title: Article 48A
bare_text: Protection and improvement of environment and safeguarding of forests and wild life.
plain_english: The state must protect the environment, forests, and wildlife of the country.
example: The creation of National Parks and the passing of the Wildlife Protection Act.

title: Article 49
bare_text: Protection of monuments and places and objects of national importance.
plain_english: It is the obligation of the state to protect every monument or place of historic interest from destruction or export.
example: The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) protecting the Taj Mahal.

title: Article 50
bare_text: Separation of judiciary from executive.
plain_english: The state shall take steps to separate the judiciary from the executive in the public services of the State.
example: A District Collector (Executive) should not also sit as a Judge to decide civil/criminal cases (Judiciary).
extra_content: • Ensures judicial independence at the grassroots level.

title: Article 51A: Fundamental Duties
bare_text: It shall be the duty of every citizen of India... to abide by the Constitution, cherish noble ideals, protect sovereignty...
plain_english: A list of 11 moral obligations of all citizens to help promote patriotism and uphold the unity of India. They are not legally enforceable.
example: It is your duty to respect the National Flag and the National Anthem, and to protect the environment.
extra_content:
• Added by the 42nd Amendment (1976) on the recommendation of the Swaran Singh Committee.
  • The 11th duty (education for children 6-14) was added by the 86th Amendment.

title: Article 51
bare_text: Promotion of international peace and security.
plain_english: The state shall endeavor to promote international peace, maintain just and honorable relations between nations, and foster respect for international law.
example: India's non-aligned foreign policy and its contributions to UN Peacekeeping missions.

title: Article 52
bare_text: The President of India.
plain_english: There shall be a President of India. (This is the shortest and most absolute article in the Constitution).
example: The office of the President can never remain vacant.
extra_content: • The President is the head of the Indian State and the first citizen of India.

title: Article 53
bare_text: Executive power of the Union.
plain_english: All executive actions of the Government of India are formally taken in the name of the President. He is also the Supreme Commander of the Armed Forces.
example: When India signs a treaty with another country, it is signed in the name of the President.
extra_content: • Though he holds the power, Article 74 requires him to exercise it on the advice of the PM.

title: Article 54
bare_text: Election of President.
plain_english: The President is elected by an Electoral College consisting of: elected members of both Houses of Parliament, and elected members of State Legislative Assemblies.
example: Nominated members (like those in Rajya Sabha) and members of State Legislative Councils cannot vote for the President.
extra_content: • Includes assemblies of Delhi and Puducherry (added by 70th Amendment).

title: Article 55
bare_text: Manner of election of President.
plain_english: The election is held in accordance with the system of proportional representation by means of the single transferable vote, using a secret ballot.
example: To win, a candidate must secure a fixed quota of votes, not just a simple majority.
extra_content: • Value of an MLA's vote varies by state population. Value of an MP's vote is uniform.

title: Article 56
bare_text: Term of office of President.
plain_english: The President holds office for a term of five years. They can resign by writing to the Vice-President.
example: Even if the 5-year term expires, the President continues to hold office until the successor takes charge.
extra_content: • Can be removed early only by impeachment (Article 61).

title: Article 57
bare_text: Eligibility for re-election.
plain_english: A person who holds, or who has held, office as President is eligible for re-election to that office any number of times.
example: Dr. Rajendra Prasad is the only President to have been elected for two terms.
extra_content: • Unlike the US Constitution, which limits Presidents to two terms.

title: Article 58
bare_text: Qualifications for election as President.
plain_english: To be President, you must be a citizen of India, at least 35 years old, qualified to be a Lok Sabha member, and not hold any office of profit.
example: A sitting Governor or Minister is not considered to hold an 'office of profit' for this purpose and can run for President.
extra_content: • A naturalized citizen can become President in India (unlike the USA where they must be natural-born).

title: Article 59
bare_text: Conditions of President's office.
plain_english: The President cannot be an MP or MLA. If they are, they must vacate their seat. Their salary and allowances cannot be reduced during their term.
example: If a sitting MP is elected President, they instantly stop being an MP the moment they enter the Rashtrapati Bhavan.
extra_content: • Enjoys personal immunity from legal liability for official acts.

title: Article 60
bare_text: Oath or affirmation by the President.
plain_english: Before entering office, the President takes an oath to 'preserve, protect and defend the Constitution and the law'.
example: The oath is administered by the Chief Justice of India (CJI).
extra_content: • If the CJI is absent, the senior-most judge of the Supreme Court administers the oath.

title: Article 61
bare_text: Procedure for impeachment of the President.
plain_english: The President can be removed from office for 'violation of the Constitution'. The impeachment must be passed by a 2/3rds majority of the total membership of BOTH houses.
example: No President of India has ever been impeached.
extra_content: • Nominated members of Parliament CAN vote in the impeachment (unlike the election). MLAs cannot vote.

title: Article 62
bare_text: Time of holding election to fill vacancy in the office of President.
plain_english: An election to fill a vacancy caused by the expiration of the term must be completed BEFORE the term expires. If the vacancy is due to death or resignation, it must be held within 6 months.
example: When President Zakir Husain died in office, elections for a new President had to be held within 6 months.
extra_content: • The Vice-President acts as President during the 6-month vacancy period.

title: Article 63
bare_text: The Vice-President of India.
plain_english: There shall be a Vice-President of India.
example: This is the second-highest office in the country, modeled on the American Vice-President.
extra_content: • However, unlike the US VP, the Indian VP does not assume the Presidency for the rest of the term if the President dies, but only acts as President until a new one is elected.

title: Article 64
bare_text: The Vice-President to be ex officio Chairman of the Council of States.
plain_english: The Vice-President is automatically the Chairman (Speaker) of the Rajya Sabha.
example: Whenever the Rajya Sabha is in session, the Vice-President presides over the meetings.
extra_content: • The VP draws his regular salary in his capacity as the Chairman of the Rajya Sabha, not as VP.

title: Article 65
bare_text: The Vice-President to act as President or to discharge his functions during casual vacancies in the office, or during the absence, of President.
plain_english: If the President dies, resigns, or is removed, the VP acts as President for a maximum of 6 months until a new election is held.
example: When President Fakhruddin Ali Ahmed died, VP B.D. Jatti acted as President.
extra_content: • While acting as President, the VP gets all the powers, salary, and immunities of the President, and ceases to act as Rajya Sabha Chairman.

title: Article 66
bare_text: Election of Vice-President.
plain_english: Elected by an electoral college of ALL members (elected and nominated) of BOTH houses of Parliament. MLAs do not vote.
example: An Anglo-Indian nominated member in the Lok Sabha (historically) could vote for the VP, but not the President.
extra_content: • Must be 35 years old and qualified for election as a Rajya Sabha member.

title: Article 67
bare_text: Term of office of Vice-President.
plain_english: Holds office for 5 years. Can be removed by a resolution passed by a majority of all the then members of the Rajya Sabha and agreed to by the Lok Sabha.
example: Unlike the President, the VP does not require a formal 'impeachment' with a 2/3rds majority in both houses.
extra_content: • No ground for removal is mentioned in the Constitution.

title: Article 68
bare_text: Time of holding election to fill vacancy in the office of Vice-President.
plain_english: Elections for a normal vacancy must be held before the term expires. For an unexpected vacancy (death/resignation), an election must be held 'as soon as possible'.
example: There is no strict 6-month limit for the VP election like there is for the President.
extra_content: • The newly elected VP serves a full 5-year term.

title: Article 69
bare_text: Oath or affirmation by the Vice-President.
plain_english: The VP takes an oath to bear true faith and allegiance to the Constitution.
example: The oath is administered by the President (unlike the President's oath, which is administered by the CJI).
extra_content: • Or some person appointed by the President.

title: Article 70
bare_text: Discharge of President's functions in other contingencies.
plain_english: Parliament can make laws for discharging the President's functions in unforeseen situations not mentioned in the Constitution.
example: Under this, Parliament passed the President (Discharge of Functions) Act 1969, stating that if both President and VP offices are vacant, the Chief Justice of India acts as President.
extra_content: • Justice M. Hidayatullah is the only CJI to have acted as President under this contingency law.

title: Article 71
bare_text: Matters relating to, or connected with, the election of a President or Vice-President.
plain_english: Any dispute regarding the election of the President or Vice-President is decided EXCLUSIVELY by the Supreme Court.
example: If a losing candidate claims the President's election was rigged, they must file a petition directly in the Supreme Court.
extra_content:
• The decision of the Supreme Court is final.
  • If the election is declared void, acts done by the President prior to that declaration are NOT invalidated.

title: Article 72: Pardoning Power of the President
bare_text: The President shall have the power to grant pardons, reprieves, respites or remissions of punishment or to suspend, remit or commute the sentence of any person convicted of any offence...
plain_english: The President has the power to forgive criminals, reduce their sentences, or change a death sentence to life imprisonment.
example: A death row inmate can send a mercy petition to the President as a last resort.
extra_content:
• Applies to offenses against Union law, Court Martials, and all death sentences.
  • The Governor (Art 161) also has pardoning power, but cannot pardon a death sentence or Court Martial.

title: Article 73
bare_text: Extent of executive power of the Union.
plain_english: The executive power of the Central Government extends only to those matters on which Parliament has the power to make laws.
example: The Central Government cannot arbitrarily execute policies on purely State List subjects (like local police) unless a specific constitutional exception applies.
extra_content: • Aligns executive action strictly with legislative competence.

title: Article 74: Council of Ministers to aid and advise President
bare_text: There shall be a Council of Ministers with the Prime Minister at the head to aid and advise the President who shall, in the exercise of his functions, act in accordance with such advice.
plain_english: The President is just a nominal head. He MUST act on the advice of the Prime Minister and the Cabinet.
example: If the PM advises the President to dissolve the Lok Sabha, the President has to do it.
extra_content:
• The 42nd Amendment made the advice binding.
  • The 44th Amendment allowed the President to send the advice back for reconsideration exactly once.

title: Article 75
bare_text: Other provisions as to Ministers.
plain_english: The Prime Minister is appointed by the President, and other Ministers are appointed on the PM's advice. The entire Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha.
example: If a No-Confidence Motion is passed in the Lok Sabha, the entire Council of Ministers (including the PM) must resign.
extra_content:
• Establishes the core principle of Parliamentary Democracy (collective responsibility).
  • Total number of ministers cannot exceed 15% of the total strength of the Lok Sabha (91st Amendment).

title: Article 76
bare_text: Attorney-General for India.
plain_english: The President appoints the Attorney General, who is the highest law officer in the country and gives legal advice to the Government of India.
example: When the Central Government is sued in the Supreme Court, the Attorney General represents them.
extra_content:
• Must be qualified to be a Supreme Court judge.
  • Has the right to speak in both Houses of Parliament but cannot vote (Article 88).

title: Article 77
bare_text: Conduct of business of the Government of India.
plain_english: Every executive action of the Government of India must be expressed to be taken in the name of the President.
example: Government orders and notifications are signed 'By order of the President'.
extra_content: • The President makes rules for the convenient transaction of government business.

title: Article 78
bare_text: Duties of Prime Minister as respects the furnishing of information to the President, etc.
plain_english: It is the constitutional duty of the Prime Minister to communicate all decisions of the Cabinet to the President and furnish any information the President asks for.
example: The President can demand to know what the Cabinet decided regarding a new controversial agricultural policy.
extra_content: • The PM acts as the sole channel of communication between the Cabinet and the President.

title: Article 79
bare_text: Constitution of Parliament.
plain_english: The Parliament of India consists of three parts: The President, the Council of States (Rajya Sabha), and the House of the People (Lok Sabha).
example: Even though the President doesn't sit in the Lok Sabha, they are an integral part of Parliament because no bill can become law without their signature.
extra_content: • Modeled on the British system (Crown + House of Lords + House of Commons) rather than the American system where the President is separate from Congress.

title: Article 80
bare_text: Composition of the Council of States.
plain_english: The Rajya Sabha can have a maximum of 250 members (238 elected by state assemblies, 12 nominated by the President).
example: The President nominates famous personalities (like Sachin Tendulkar or Mary Kom) who have special knowledge in literature, science, art, or social service.
extra_content: • Represents the states in the federal system.

title: Article 81
bare_text: Composition of the House of the People.
plain_english: The Lok Sabha consists of members directly elected by the public from territorial constituencies.
example: During a general election, citizens directly vote for their local Member of Parliament (MP) to sit in the Lok Sabha.
extra_content: • Maximum strength is currently 550. (The provision for 2 nominated Anglo-Indians was removed by the 104th Amendment).

title: Article 82
bare_text: Readjustment after each census.
plain_english: After every census, the boundaries of electoral constituencies are redrawn (delimitation) to ensure equal population representation.
example: A Delimitation Commission is formed to adjust borders so that every MP represents roughly the same number of people.
extra_content: • The number of seats in the Lok Sabha has been frozen at the 1971 census level until the year 2026 to encourage population control in southern states.

title: Article 83
bare_text: Duration of Houses of Parliament.
plain_english: The Rajya Sabha is a permanent body and cannot be dissolved, but 1/3rd of its members retire every 2 years. The Lok Sabha lasts for 5 years unless dissolved earlier.
example: If the PM loses a majority, the President can dissolve the Lok Sabha before its 5-year term ends and call for fresh elections.
extra_content: • During a National Emergency, the Lok Sabha's term can be extended by one year at a time.

title: Article 84
bare_text: Qualification for membership of Parliament.
plain_english: To be an MP, you must be a citizen of India, at least 30 years old for Rajya Sabha, and at least 25 years old for Lok Sabha.
example: A 26-year-old politician can run for Lok Sabha but is too young to run for Rajya Sabha.
extra_content: • Must make an oath before the Election Commission.

title: Article 85
bare_text: Sessions of Parliament, prorogation and dissolution.
plain_english: The President summons (calls) and prorogues (ends) the sessions of Parliament. The maximum gap between two sessions cannot be more than 6 months.
example: Parliament usually meets in three sessions: Budget Session, Monsoon Session, and Winter Session.
extra_content: • 'Prorogation' ends the session, while 'Dissolution' ends the life of the Lok Sabha itself.

title: Article 86
bare_text: Right of President to address and send messages to Houses.
plain_english: The President can address either House or both Houses together, and can send messages regarding pending bills.
example: The President may send a message to Parliament urging them to quickly pass an important, stalled anti-corruption bill.
extra_content: • This is a discretionary power of the President.

title: Article 87
bare_text: Special address by the President.
plain_english: The President MUST address both Houses assembled together at the start of the first session after every general election, and at the start of the first session of every year.
example: The President's address outlines the government's policies and upcoming legislative agenda (usually drafted by the Cabinet).
extra_content: • Following the address, a 'Motion of Thanks' is debated and voted upon. If it fails, the government must resign.

title: Article 88
bare_text: Rights of Ministers and Attorney-General as respects Houses.
plain_english: Any Minister and the Attorney General have the right to speak and take part in the proceedings of either House, but the Attorney General cannot vote.
example: A Minister who is a member of the Rajya Sabha can speak in the Lok Sabha to defend a bill, but can only vote in the Rajya Sabha.

title: Article 89
bare_text: The Chairman and Deputy Chairman of the Council of States.
plain_english: The Vice-President is the ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha. The Rajya Sabha also elects a Deputy Chairman from among its members.
example: If the Vice-President is acting as President (or is absent), the Deputy Chairman presides over the Rajya Sabha.
extra_content: • The Chairman is NOT a member of the House, but the Deputy Chairman is.

title: Article 93
bare_text: The Speaker and Deputy Speaker of the House of the People.
plain_english: The Lok Sabha elects two of its members to be the Speaker and the Deputy Speaker as soon as possible after a new election.
example: The Speaker maintains order in the house, punishes unruly MPs, and decides who gets to speak.
extra_content: • By convention, the Speaker is usually from the ruling party, and the Deputy Speaker is from the opposition.

title: Article 98
bare_text: Secretariat of Parliament.
plain_english: Each House of Parliament has its own separate secretarial staff (bureaucrats and clerks) independent of the executive government.
example: The Secretary-General of the Lok Sabha works for the Parliament, not for the Prime Minister.

title: Article 99
bare_text: Oath or affirmation by members.
plain_english: Every Member of Parliament (MP) must take an oath before the President (or a person appointed by him) before taking their seat.
example: Usually, the President appoints a 'Pro-tem Speaker' to administer the oath to all newly elected MPs.
extra_content: • If an MP votes in the house without taking the oath, they face a financial penalty of Rs. 500 per day.

title: Article 100
bare_text: Voting in Houses, power of Houses to act notwithstanding vacancies and quorum.
plain_english: All questions in the house are decided by a majority vote. The Speaker does not vote in the first instance, but casts a deciding vote in case of a tie. The 'quorum' (minimum members needed to hold a meeting) is 10% of the total members.
example: If the Lok Sabha has 550 members, at least 55 must be present for a session to happen. If there's a 100-100 tie on a bill, the Speaker casts the final vote.
extra_content: • The casting vote of the Speaker is to maintain impartiality.

title: Article 101
bare_text: Vacation of seats.
plain_english: A person cannot be a member of both Houses of Parliament, or a member of both Parliament and a State Legislature. If an MP is absent for 60 days without permission, their seat can be declared vacant.
example: If an MLA wins a Lok Sabha election, they must resign from the state assembly, otherwise their Parliament seat becomes vacant.

title: Article 102
bare_text: Disqualifications for membership.
plain_english: An MP can be disqualified if they hold an 'office of profit', become mentally unsound, go bankrupt, lose citizenship, or are disqualified under the Anti-Defection Law (10th Schedule).
example: If an MP accepts a salaried job as the director of a government corporation, they will be disqualified for holding an office of profit.
extra_content: • The President decides disqualifications based on the Election Commission's advice. Disqualification for defection is decided by the Speaker.

title: Article 105
bare_text: Powers, privileges, etc., of the Houses of Parliament and of the members and committees thereof.
plain_english: MPs have special privileges, like absolute freedom of speech inside Parliament. They cannot be sued or arrested for anything they say or any vote they cast in the House.
example: An MP can expose a massive corporate scam on the floor of the House without fear of being sued for defamation.
extra_content: • They also have freedom from civil arrest 40 days before and after a parliamentary session.

title: Article 106
bare_text: [Official Text for Article 106 will be synchronized with the Ministry of Law and Justice database.]
plain_english: The plain English explanation for this specific article is currently being drafted and verified by our legal experts.

title: Article 107
bare_text: Provisions as to introduction and passing of Bills.
plain_english: A bill (except Money or Financial Bills) can be introduced in either House. It must be passed by BOTH Houses to become a law.
example: If a bill is introduced in the Lok Sabha, it must also go to the Rajya Sabha and be passed there before going to the President.
extra_content: • A bill pending in Parliament does not lapse just because the houses are prorogued (ended for the session).

title: Article 108
bare_text: Joint sitting of both Houses in certain cases.
plain_english: If the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha cannot agree on an ordinary bill for 6 months, the President can summon a 'joint sitting' of both houses to resolve the deadlock by a simple majority vote.
example: Because the Lok Sabha has more than double the members of the Rajya Sabha, the Lok Sabha usually wins in a joint sitting.
extra_content:
• The Speaker of the Lok Sabha presides over the joint sitting.
  • Joint sittings CANNOT be held for Money Bills or Constitutional Amendment Bills.

title: Article 109
bare_text: Special procedure in respect of Money Bills.
plain_english: A Money Bill can ONLY be introduced in the Lok Sabha. The Rajya Sabha cannot reject it or amend it; they can only delay it for a maximum of 14 days.
example: If the Rajya Sabha hates a tax bill, they can send it back with recommendations, but the Lok Sabha can just ignore the recommendations and pass it anyway.
extra_content: • Establishes the absolute supremacy of the Lok Sabha in financial matters.

title: Article 110: Definition of 'Money Bills'
bare_text: A Bill shall be deemed to be a Money Bill if it contains only provisions dealing with... the imposition, abolition, remission, alteration or regulation of any tax...
plain_english: A Money Bill is a special type of law dealing only with taxes, borrowing money, or the Consolidated Fund.
example: The annual Union Budget and tax changes are passed as Money Bills.
extra_content:
• Can ONLY be introduced in the Lok Sabha.
  • The Speaker's decision on whether a bill is a Money Bill is final.
  • Rajya Sabha can only delay it for 14 days.

title: Article 111
bare_text: Assent to Bills.
plain_english: When a bill is passed by both Houses, it goes to the President. The President can sign it, withhold signature (veto), or send it back for reconsideration (unless it's a Money Bill).
example: If the President sends a bill back, and Parliament passes it a second time (with or without changes), the President MUST sign it.

title: Article 112
bare_text: Annual financial statement.
plain_english: This is the Constitutional term for the 'Union Budget'. The President must ensure that the estimated receipts and expenditures of the government are laid before Parliament every financial year.
example: The Finance Minister reads the Budget in February, fulfilling the requirement of Article 112.
extra_content: • The word 'Budget' is never actually used in the Constitution.

title: Article 114
bare_text: Appropriation Bills.
plain_english: The government cannot withdraw a single rupee from the Consolidated Fund of India unless Parliament passes an Appropriation Bill authorizing it.
example: Even if the Budget is approved, the money can't legally leave the treasury until the Appropriation Bill is formally passed.
extra_content: • This ensures total parliamentary control over the public purse.

title: Article 123: Ordinance making power of the President
bare_text: If at any time, except when both Houses of Parliament are in session, the President is satisfied that circumstances exist which render it necessary for him to take immediate action, he may promulgate such Ordinances...
plain_english: When Parliament is on vacation, the President can issue an 'Ordinance' which has the exact same power as a regular law. It's for emergencies.
example: The government urgently banned Triple Talaq via an Ordinance before it was formally passed as an Act in Parliament.
extra_content:
• Must be approved by Parliament within 6 weeks of its reassembly.
  • Maximum life of an ordinance is 6 months and 6 weeks.

title: Article 124
bare_text: Establishment and constitution of Supreme Court.
plain_english: Establishes the Supreme Court of India. Contains the rules for appointing Judges (by the President) and removing them (by impeachment in Parliament).
example: A Supreme Court judge retires at the age of 65. To remove them earlier requires a 2/3rds majority in both houses.
extra_content: • The controversial 'Collegium System' for appointing judges evolved from the interpretation of the word 'consultation' in this article.

title: Article 129
bare_text: Supreme Court to be a court of record.
plain_english: The Supreme Court is a 'Court of Record', meaning its judgments are recorded for perpetual memory and act as binding precedents for all lower courts. It also has the power to punish people for 'Contempt of Court'.
example: If a lawyer insults a Supreme Court judge during a hearing, the Court can send them to jail for contempt.
extra_content: • Contempt can be civil (disobeying an order) or criminal (scandalizing the court).

title: Article 130
bare_text: [Official Text for Article 130 will be synchronized with the Ministry of Law and Justice database.]

title: Article 131
bare_text: Original jurisdiction of the Supreme Court.
plain_english: The Supreme Court has EXCLUSIVE power to hear disputes between the Central Government and States, or between two different States.
example: If the state of Karnataka sues the state of Tamil Nadu over river water sharing (though usually handled by tribunals, pure federal disputes go here).
extra_content: • A regular citizen cannot use Article 131 to sue a state; it is strictly for federal disputes.

title: Article 132
bare_text: Appellate jurisdiction of Supreme Court in appeals from High Courts in certain cases.
plain_english: You can appeal a High Court judgment to the Supreme Court if the High Court certifies that the case involves a 'substantial question of law as to the interpretation of the Constitution'.
example: If a High Court rules that a new surveillance law is constitutional, the losing party can appeal to the SC because it involves constitutional interpretation.
extra_content: • The SC is the ultimate interpreter of the Constitution.

title: Article 136
bare_text: Special leave to appeal by the Supreme Court.
plain_english: The Supreme Court can grant 'Special Leave' to appeal against ANY judgment passed by ANY court or tribunal in India (except military courts).
example: Even if a High Court refuses to let you appeal, you can directly ask the Supreme Court to hear your case using an SLP (Special Leave Petition).
extra_content: • This is a discretionary power of the SC, not a right of the citizen. It is the most invoked article for appeals.

title: Article 137
bare_text: Review of judgments or orders by the Supreme Court.
plain_english: The Supreme Court has the power to review its own past judgments and change its mind.
example: In the Golaknath case, the SC said Parliament cannot amend Fundamental Rights. Years later, in the Kesavananda Bharati case, they reviewed and reversed that decision.
extra_content: • Ensures that the apex court can correct its own errors.

title: Article 141
bare_text: Law declared by Supreme Court to be binding on all courts.
plain_english: Whatever the Supreme Court decides becomes the absolute law of the land and MUST be followed by every single High Court and lower court in India.
example: If the SC rules that a specific type of contract is illegal, a lower court in a small district cannot rule otherwise in a similar case.
extra_content: • This creates a unified judicial system across the entire country.

title: Article 142
bare_text: Enforcement of decrees and orders of Supreme Court and orders as to discovery, etc.
plain_english: The Supreme Court can pass ANY order necessary to do 'complete justice' in a case, even if there is no specific law for it.
example: The SC used Article 142 to ban liquor shops on national highways, and to order the transfer of the Babri Masjid land to a trust, to ensure 'complete justice'.
extra_content: • This is the source of the SC's immense, almost limitless 'judicial activism' power.

title: Article 143: Power of President to consult Supreme Court
bare_text: If at any time it appears to the President that a question of law or fact has arisen, or is likely to arise, which is of such a nature and of such public importance that it is expedient to obtain the opinion of the Supreme Court...
plain_english: The President can ask the Supreme Court for legal advice on important public matters. This is the 'Advisory Jurisdiction'.
example: The President asked the SC for its opinion regarding the Ram Janmabhoomi dispute and the sharing of Cauvery water.
extra_content:
• The Supreme Court is not bound to give its opinion.
  • The President is not bound to accept the opinion.

title: Article 148
bare_text: Comptroller and Auditor-General of India.
plain_english: Creates the office of the CAG, the independent supreme auditor of the country. They are appointed by the President and can only be removed like a Supreme Court judge.
example: The CAG is the one who audits government accounts and exposes massive financial scandals (like the 2G scam or Coal scam).
extra_content: • The CAG acts as the guardian of the public purse.

title: Article 149
bare_text: Duties and powers of the Comptroller and Auditor-General.
plain_english: The CAG audits the accounts of the Union Government, State Governments, and any other authority as prescribed by Parliament.
example: The CAG audits how much money the defense ministry actually spent on purchasing fighter jets compared to what was budgeted.
extra_content: • The CAG submits these audit reports to the President/Governor, who lays them before Parliament/State Legislature.

title: Article 153
bare_text: Governors of States.
plain_english: There shall be a Governor for each State. However, the same person can be appointed as the Governor of two or more States.
example: The Governor of Maharashtra is often also given additional charge of Goa.
extra_content: • The 7th Amendment (1956) facilitated the appointment of the same person as Governor for multiple states.

title: Article 154
bare_text: Executive power of State.
plain_english: Just like the President is the executive head of the Union (Art 53), the Governor is the executive head of the State. All state executive actions are taken in their name.
example: When the State Government of Kerala signs an order, it is signed 'By order of the Governor'.
extra_content: • The Governor acts on the advice of the Chief Minister and the state cabinet.

title: Article 155
bare_text: Appointment of Governor.
plain_english: The Governor is NOT elected by the people. They are directly appointed by the President by warrant under his hand and seal.
example: Because they are appointed by the President (on the advice of the PM), the Governor acts as a vital link (and sometimes a point of friction) between the Centre and the State.
extra_content: • India rejected the American system of electing state governors to maintain tighter central control.

title: Article 156
bare_text: Term of office of Governor.
plain_english: The Governor holds office 'during the pleasure of the President'. Usually, the term is 5 years, but the President can dismiss them at any time without giving a reason.
example: When a new political party comes to power at the Centre, they often instantly fire and replace the Governors in all the states.
extra_content: • The Supreme Court has ruled that this dismissal cannot be purely arbitrary, but it remains a highly politicized process.

title: Article 161
bare_text: Power of Governor to grant pardons, etc., and to suspend, remit or commute sentences in certain cases.
plain_english: The Governor can pardon or reduce the sentence of any person convicted of a crime against a State Law.
example: A Governor can remit (reduce) the life sentence of a prisoner in a state jail for good behavior.
extra_content: • **Crucial Difference from President:** The Governor CANNOT pardon a death sentence (only the President can under Art 72), and cannot pardon Court Martial sentences.

title: Article 163
bare_text: Council of Ministers to aid and advise Governor.
plain_english: The Governor must act on the advice of the Chief Minister and the State Cabinet, except in matters where the Constitution gives the Governor 'discretion'.
example: Unlike the President (Art 74), the Governor actually has some discretionary powers, such as recommending President's Rule under Article 356.
extra_content: • If a question arises whether a matter falls under the Governor's discretion, the Governor's decision is final.

title: Article 164
bare_text: Other provisions as to Ministers.
plain_english: The Chief Minister is appointed by the Governor. The Ministers hold office 'during the pleasure of the Governor', but are collectively responsible to the State Legislative Assembly.
example: If the state government loses a floor test in the Assembly, the entire Council of Ministers must resign.
extra_content: • In states like Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, MP, and Odisha, there must be a minister in charge of tribal welfare.

title: Article 165
bare_text: Advocate-General for the State.
plain_english: Just like the Attorney General at the Centre, every State has an Advocate General appointed by the Governor to give legal advice to the state government.
example: When the State of Punjab is sued in the High Court, the Advocate General represents the state.
extra_content: • Must be qualified to be a High Court judge.

title: Article 167
bare_text: Duties of Chief Minister as respects the furnishing of information to Governor, etc.
plain_english: The Chief Minister must communicate all cabinet decisions to the Governor and furnish any information regarding state administration that the Governor asks for.
example: The Governor can demand an update from the CM regarding the state's handling of a severe riot.
extra_content: • Identical to the Prime Minister's duty to the President under Article 78.

title: Article 169
bare_text: Abolition or creation of Legislative Councils in States.
plain_english: Parliament can create or abolish a State Legislative Council (Vidhan Parishad) if the State Legislative Assembly passes a special resolution asking them to do so.
example: Andhra Pradesh passed a resolution in their assembly to abolish their Legislative Council, but the final power to abolish it lies with the Parliament in Delhi.
extra_content: • Only 6 states currently have a Legislative Council (Bicameral legislature).

title: Article 170
bare_text: Composition of the Legislative Assemblies.
plain_english: A State Legislative Assembly (Vidhan Sabha) must have a maximum of 500 members and a minimum of 60 members, directly elected by the public.
example: Uttar Pradesh has the largest assembly (403 members), while smaller states like Sikkim have exceptions to the 60-member minimum rule.
extra_content: • Seats are reserved for SCs and STs based on their population ratio.

title: Article 171
bare_text: Composition of the Legislative Councils.
plain_english: The total number of members in the Legislative Council cannot exceed 1/3rd of the members in the Assembly. They are elected indirectly by teachers, graduates, MLAs, and local bodies.
example: The Governor also nominates members (like artists or scientists) to the Council.
extra_content: • Acts as a revising chamber, similar to the Rajya Sabha, but with far less power.

title: Article 197
bare_text: Restriction on powers of Legislative Council as to Bills other than Money Bills.
plain_english: Unlike the Rajya Sabha, the State Legislative Council cannot block an ordinary bill. It can only delay it for a maximum of 4 months (3 months first time + 1 month second time).
example: If the UP Assembly passes a bill and the Council rejects it, the Assembly can just pass it a second time and it bypasses the Council entirely.
extra_content: • Shows the utter supremacy of the Legislative Assembly over the Council.

title: Article 200
bare_text: Assent to Bills.
plain_english: When a bill is passed by the state legislature, it goes to the Governor. The Governor can sign it, withhold assent, return it, or RESERVE it for the President's consideration.
example: If a state passes a bill that threatens the powers of the High Court, the Governor MUST reserve it for the President to review.
extra_content: • This is a crucial tool of central control over state legislation.

title: Article 201
bare_text: Bills reserved for consideration.
plain_english: When a bill is reserved by the Governor, the President can either give assent or direct the Governor to return it to the state legislature.
example: The President has absolute veto power over state bills reserved for him; there is no time limit for him to decide.
extra_content: • The state legislature cannot override the President's veto.

title: Article 213
bare_text: Power of Governor to promulgate Ordinances during recess of Legislature.
plain_english: The Governor can issue Ordinances (temporary laws) when the state legislature is not in session, just like the President does under Article 123.
example: A state government urgently passing a law to ban an addictive substance via an ordinance before the assembly meets.
extra_content:
• Must be approved by the state legislature within 6 weeks of reassembly.
  • For certain subjects, the Governor needs prior instructions from the President.

title: Article 214
bare_text: High Courts for States.
plain_english: There shall be a High Court for each State.
example: The Allahabad High Court is the highest judicial authority in Uttar Pradesh.
extra_content: • Parliament can establish a common High Court for two or more states (Article 231), like the Punjab and Haryana High Court.

title: Article 215
bare_text: High Courts to be courts of record.
plain_english: Like the Supreme Court, every High Court is a 'Court of Record' and has the power to punish for contempt of itself.
example: The judgments of the Bombay High Court are binding on all lower district courts in Maharashtra.

bare_text: Appointment and conditions of the office of a Judge of a High Court.

example: A High Court judge retires at the age of 62 (unlike SC judges who retire at 65).
extra_content: • They can only be removed through the same difficult impeachment process as an SC judge.

title: Article 226: Power of High Courts to issue certain writs
bare_text: Power of High Courts to issue certain writs.
plain_english: The High Court can issue writs (Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, etc.) to enforce Fundamental Rights AND for 'any other purpose' (like ordinary legal rights).
example: If your passport is arbitrarily revoked, you can file a writ petition in the High Court under Article 226.
extra_content: • The writ jurisdiction of the High Court is actually WIDER than that of the Supreme Court (which can only issue writs for Fundamental Rights under Art 32).

title: Article 227
bare_text: Power of superintendence over all courts by the High Court.
plain_english: The High Court has administrative and judicial control over all subordinate courts and tribunals throughout its territorial jurisdiction.
example: The High Court can call for records from a lower District Court to ensure they are following the rules.
extra_content: • Excludes courts/tribunals constituted under any law relating to the Armed Forces.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 245
bare_text: Extent of laws made by Parliament and by the Legislatures of States.
plain_english: Parliament can make laws for the whole or any part of India, and its laws can apply to Indian citizens anywhere in the world (extraterritorial operation). State legislatures can only make laws for their specific state.
example: If Parliament bans cybercrime, an Indian citizen hacking from London can still be prosecuted under that law. A law passed by the Gujarat assembly only applies within Gujarat.
extra_content: • Establishes the fundamental territorial limits of legislative power.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 246
bare_text: Subject-matter of laws made by Parliament and by the Legislatures of States.
plain_english: Divides law-making power into three lists (Seventh Schedule): Union List (only Parliament can make laws), State List (only States can make laws), and Concurrent List (both can make laws).
example: Defense is in the Union List. Police is in the State List. Education is in the Concurrent List.
extra_content: • The core article establishing the federal distribution of powers.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 248
bare_text: Residuary powers of legislation.
plain_english: If a completely new subject arises that is NOT mentioned in the Union, State, or Concurrent lists, ONLY the Parliament has the power to make laws on it.
example: When the internet was invented, laws regarding Information Technology (which wasn't in any list) were made exclusively by Parliament using its residuary power.
extra_content: • Unlike the US Constitution, where residuary powers belong to the states.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 249
bare_text: Power of Parliament to legislate with respect to a matter in the State List in the national interest.
plain_english: If the Rajya Sabha passes a resolution with a 2/3rds majority declaring it necessary in the 'national interest', Parliament can temporarily make laws on subjects in the State List.
example: If agricultural supply chains break down nationally, Rajya Sabha can authorize Parliament to pass a temporary nationwide agricultural law.
extra_content: • Demonstrates how the Indian Constitution can act like a unitary system during normal times.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 254
bare_text: Inconsistency between laws made by Parliament and laws made by the Legislatures of States.
plain_english: If both Parliament and a State make a law on a Concurrent List subject and they contradict each other, the Central law prevails and the State law becomes void.
example: If the Centre says environmental clearance takes 30 days, and a State says 90 days, the Central law overrides the state law.
extra_content: • Exception: If the state law was reserved for and received the President's assent, it prevails in that specific state.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 262
bare_text: Adjudication of disputes relating to waters of inter-State rivers or river valleys.
plain_english: Parliament can make laws to resolve disputes between states over river waters. Parliament can also completely exclude the Supreme Court from interfering in these disputes.
example: The setting up of the Cauvery Water Disputes Tribunal between Karnataka and Tamil Nadu.
extra_content: • Inter-State Water Disputes Act, 1956 was passed under this article.

category: Part XI: Relations between Union and States
title: Article 263
bare_text: Provisions with respect to an inter-State Council.
plain_english: The President can establish an Inter-State Council to investigate disputes between states and discuss subjects of common interest.
example: Used to coordinate policies regarding health, education, and internal security across all states.
extra_content: • Set up permanently in 1990 based on the Sarkaria Commission recommendations.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 265
bare_text: Taxes not to be imposed save by authority of law.
plain_english: No tax can be levied or collected by the government except by the authority of a law passed by the legislature.
example: A police officer or a minister cannot arbitrarily demand a 'road tax' from you unless the Legislative Assembly has officially passed a law creating that tax.
extra_content: • A foundational principle protecting citizens from arbitrary extortion by the executive.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 266
bare_text: Consolidated Funds and public accounts of India and of the States.
plain_english: Creates the 'Consolidated Fund of India' into which ALL revenues received, loans raised, and money recovered by the government must be deposited.
example: Every single rupee collected as Income Tax goes directly into the Consolidated Fund.
extra_content: • No money can be withdrawn from this fund without Parliamentary approval (Appropriation Bill).

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 267
bare_text: Contingency Fund.
plain_english: Creates an emergency fund placed at the disposal of the President to meet unforeseen, urgent expenditures without waiting for Parliamentary approval.
example: If a massive earthquake hits, the President can immediately release funds for rescue operations from the Contingency Fund.
extra_content: • The fund is later replenished from the Consolidated Fund after Parliament retroactively approves it.

category: Part XII: Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits
title: Article 300A
bare_text: Persons not to be deprived of property save by authority of law.
plain_english: The Right to Property is no longer a Fundamental Right, but it is a Constitutional Right. The government cannot take your land unless there is a specific law authorizing it.
example: If the government wants to build a highway through your farm, they must acquire it legally under the Land Acquisition Act and pay you compensation.
extra_content: • Inserted by the 44th Amendment Act (1978) after deleting Article 31 from Fundamental Rights.

category: Part XIII: Trade, Commerce and Intercourse
title: Article 301
bare_text: Freedom of trade, commerce and intercourse.
plain_english: Trade and commerce shall be completely free throughout the territory of India. States generally cannot block goods coming from other states.
example: Maharashtra cannot ban the import of apples from Himachal Pradesh just to protect its own local farmers.
extra_content: • Ensures India remains a single, unified economic market. Subject to reasonable restrictions in the public interest.
