category: Part XIV: Services under the Union and States
title: Article 311
bare_text: Dismissal, removal or reduction in rank of persons employed in civil capacities under the Union or a State.
plain_english: Provides strong job security to civil servants. They cannot be dismissed by an authority subordinate to the one who appointed them, and they MUST be given a chance to defend themselves in an inquiry.
example: A District Magistrate (IAS officer) appointed by the President cannot be fired by the State Chief Minister.
extra_content: • A core safeguard ensuring civil servants can act independently without fear of arbitrary political firing.

category: Part XIV: Services under the Union and States
title: Article 312
bare_text: All-India services.
plain_english: If the Rajya Sabha passes a resolution by a 2/3rds majority, Parliament can create new 'All-India Services' common to both the Centre and the States.
example: The Indian Administrative Service (IAS), Indian Police Service (IPS), and Indian Forest Service (IFoS) are the only three All-India Services.
extra_content: • Officers are recruited by the Centre but serve in the State cadres.

title: Article 324
bare_text: Superintendence, direction and control of elections to be vested in an Election Commission.
plain_english: Creates the independent Election Commission of India (ECI). It has the absolute power to direct and control all elections to Parliament, State Legislatures, the President, and the Vice-President.
example: During election time, the ECI becomes incredibly powerful, taking control of the police and administration to ensure free and fair voting.
extra_content: • The Chief Election Commissioner can only be removed in the same difficult manner as a Supreme Court judge.

title: Article 326
bare_text: Elections to the House of the People and to the Legislative Assemblies of States to be on the basis of adult suffrage.
plain_english: Guarantees Universal Adult Franchise. Every Indian citizen who is at least 18 years old has the fundamental right to vote in national and state elections.
example: No citizen can be denied the right to vote based on religion, race, caste, sex, or lack of wealth.
extra_content: • The voting age was reduced from 21 to 18 by the 61st Amendment Act (1988).

example: If a constituency is reserved for SCs, only a candidate from the SC community can contest the election there (though everyone votes).

title: Article 356: President's Rule (State Emergency)
bare_text: Provisions in case of failure of constitutional machinery in States.
plain_english: Known as 'President's Rule'. If a state government cannot be carried on in accordance with the Constitution, the Centre can dismiss the state government and take direct control.
example: If a Chief Minister loses their majority in the assembly and no other party can form a government, President's Rule is imposed.
extra_content: • Historically, the most misused article in the Constitution, though the S.R. Bommai Supreme Court judgment drastically curtailed its misuse.

title: Article 358
bare_text: Suspension of provisions of article 19 during emergencies.
plain_english: When a National Emergency is declared (due to war or external aggression), all the six fundamental freedoms under Article 19 (speech, assembly, movement, etc.) are AUTOMATICALLY suspended.
example: During a war with another country, the government can ban protests or restrict press freedom without passing new laws.
extra_content: • Article 19 is NOT suspended if the emergency is declared due to 'armed rebellion' (internal).

title: Article 359
bare_text: Suspension of the enforcement of the rights conferred by Part III during emergencies.
plain_english: The President can issue an order to suspend the right to move any court to enforce other Fundamental Rights during a National Emergency.
example: If suspended, you cannot file a writ in the Supreme Court claiming your rights were violated.
extra_content: • **Crucial exception:** The rights under Article 20 (protection against arbitrary conviction) and Article 21 (right to life and liberty) CAN NEVER BE SUSPENDED.

title: Article 360
bare_text: Provisions as to financial emergency.
plain_english: If the financial stability or credit of India is threatened, the President can declare a Financial Emergency, allowing the Centre to drastically reduce salaries of government employees and judges.
example: During a severe economic collapse, the Centre could order states to slash budgets and cut minister salaries.
extra_content: • A Financial Emergency has NEVER been declared in the history of India (not even during the 1991 economic crisis).

title: Article 361
bare_text: Protection of President and Governors.
plain_english: The President and Governors have absolute legal immunity. They cannot be sued or arrested for any official act, and no criminal proceedings can be started against them while they are in office.
example: Even if the President commits a crime, the police cannot arrest them as long as they are sitting in the Rashtrapati Bhavan. They must be impeached first.
extra_content: • Civil proceedings for personal acts can only be started after giving 2 months' notice.

category: Part XX: Amendment of the Constitution
title: Article 368: Power of Parliament to amend the Constitution
bare_text: Power of Parliament to amend the Constitution and procedure therefor.
plain_english: Gives Parliament the power to amend (change, add, or delete) parts of the Constitution. Most amendments require a 'Special Majority' (2/3rds of members present and voting).
example: Changing the voting age from 21 to 18 required an amendment under Article 368.
extra_content:
• However, Parliament CANNOT amend the 'Basic Structure' of the Constitution (as ruled in the famous Kesavananda Bharati case).
  • Amendments affecting the federal structure also require ratification by half the states.

title: Article 370
bare_text: Temporary provisions with respect to the State of Jammu and Kashmir.
plain_english: Granted a special autonomous status to Jammu and Kashmir, allowing it to have its own constitution and limiting the Centre's legislative powers over it.
example: For decades, many Indian laws did not automatically apply to J&K.
extra_content: • The special status was effectively abrogated by a Presidential Order in August 2019. The article still exists in the text but is virtually inoperative.

title: Article 371
bare_text: Special provision with respect to the States of Maharashtra and Gujarat.
plain_english: The first of many 'Special Provisions' (ranging from 371A to 371J) designed to protect the unique cultural, economic, or tribal interests of specific states.
example: Article 371A grants special protections to Nagaland regarding Naga customary law, while 371J provides special status to the Hyderabad-Karnataka region.
extra_content: • Unlike 370 which was 'temporary', the 371 series provisions are generally considered 'special' and permanent accommodations for regional diversity.

title: Article 395
bare_text: Repeals.
plain_english: This is the very last article of the Constitution. It officially repealed (cancelled) the old British laws that governed India, specifically the Government of India Act 1935 and the Indian Independence Act 1947.
example: The moment the Constitution came into force on Jan 26, 1950, this article ensured India cut its final legal umbilical cord with the British Parliament.
extra_content: • The ultimate symbol of total legal sovereignty.
